card.intuition: The heart of Node.js that enables non-blocking I/O by offloading tasks to the system kernel whenever possible, then running callbacks when the results arrive.
card.analogy: A spinning carnival wheel with different sections for different types of prizes. The wheel (loop) keeps spinning, and whenever it passes the 'Timer' section or the 'I/O' section, it hands out any prizes (callbacks) that are ready.
when_to_use: Optimizing Node.js applications for maximum throughput
Understanding the difference between setImmediate, setTimeout, and process.nextTick
Debugging performance bottlenecks caused by a 'blocked' loop
gotchas: The Event Loop is single-threaded — if you run a heavy for-loop (10 billion iterations), the entire server stops responding to everyone
process.nextTick() callbacks run BEFORE the next phase of the loop, which can starve I/O if used excessively

steps.annotation: Timers phase: runs callbacks scheduled by setTimeout/setInterval whose delay has elapsed.
I/O callbacks: executes deferred I/O callbacks (e.g. errors from previous cycle).
Poll phase: retrieves new I/O events. Blocks here if queue is empty, waiting for I/O.
Check phase: setImmediate() callbacks run — always after poll, before close events.
Close callbacks: socket.on("close") and similar cleanup handlers execute.

exercise.prompt: Order these Node.js functions by when their callback will execute in the loop (assuming they are all called at the 'same' time).
exercise.starter_code:
execution_order = [
    "1. process.__________ (Immediate execution after current op)",
    "2. Promise.then (Microtask)",
    "3. ______ (next phase Poll/Check)",
    "4. set________ (next phase Check)"
]
exercise.test_code:
assert "nextTick" in execution_order[0]
assert "setTimeout" in execution_order[2]
assert "Immediate" in execution_order[3]
print("PASS")
